Q1: I tried to keep a pretty structured routine, honestly. I'd block out about 25 hours a week across my seven online courses, usually spreading it out over mornings and evenings. I'm someone who learns best by doing things with my hands, so I'd try to work through problems physically, writing things out, making diagrams. But with limited resources at home, sometimes I couldn't practice things the way I wanted to, which threw off my rhythm.
Q2: I aimed for regular times, like Monday and Wednesday mornings, Thursday evenings. But honestly, with everything going on and juggling so many courses, deadlines would sometimes force me to shift things around. I tried to stick to my schedule because it helped me feel less overwhelmed, but life doesn't always cooperate when you're working with what I've got.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I saw a notification about something new being posted, or when I knew there was a gamified task waiting for me. Those little challenges actually got me clicking in more than I expected. Sometimes I'd just log in out of habit, like checking if there was anything I needed to do, even if I wasn't sure what I was looking for.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks the gamified elements made logging in feel almost automatic. I'd want to check my progress, see if I'd unlocked anything new or completed a challenge. It became part of my morning routine, like checking messages. The points and badges gave me something tangible to work toward, which I needed because otherwise it all felt too abstract.
Q5: The videos were genuinely helpful for me. I watched a lot of them, over 250 minutes worth. Seeing concepts demonstrated visually helped me understand how to actually do things, which matters when you're a hands-on learner like me. The discussion boards were also good because I could interact with other students and that made the material feel more real.
Q6: The heavy reading materials, I'll be honest, I often skipped or skimmed those. Without being able to print things out or highlight physically, reading long PDFs on my screen just didn't stick for me. I also ignored some of the optional quizzes when I was feeling too stressed because they felt like extra pressure I couldn't handle.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate progress or when it connected to something I could actually do, not just memorize. The gamified tasks were great for this because I could see my points go up and feel like I accomplished something. If something felt abstract or disconnected from real application, I'd struggle to see the point.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford emotionally. Required ones I'd force myself through even when scared about failing. Optional ones, I'd do them if I was having a good day and felt like I had mental space, but when stress was high, they were the first thing I'd cut. The gamified optional stuff was different though, those felt rewarding enough to pursue.
Q9: Absolutely, yeah. I'd download videos and watch them offline because my internet isn't always reliable. I'd also write out notes by hand, draw diagrams, and practice problems on paper. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen, working through things physically because that's how I retain information best.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours working through problems in a notebook or practicing concepts by building little models or diagrams, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd watch a video once online, then replay it in my head while working through exercises on paper. The LMS only saw that one view, not the three hours of practice that followed.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download lecture videos and PDFs when my connection was good, then study them for days afterward without logging back in. My internet isn't the most reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed and work offline. The system probably thought I disappeared, but I was actually studying pretty intensely.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. I was scared, honestly, scared of failing, scared of not understanding material well enough. The stress built up and even though I was motivated to do well, my resilience just wasn't there. I'd log in, stare at assignments, and feel paralyzed. The workload across seven courses became crushing.
Q13: Massively. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log in but just kind of drift through without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities, like needing to help out at home because resources are tight, meant I couldn't always give the LMS my full attention. Fatigue made everything blur together and I'd complete tasks mechanically without really learning.
Q14: Yeah, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd click through videos or scroll through readings while my mind was somewhere else, worrying about exams or money or whether I was going to fail. Technically I was accessing materials, but I wasn't really there mentally. It's like my body was going through the motions but my brain had checked out.
Q15: The bigger assignments terrified me, so I'd put them off even knowing they were important. I'd tell myself I'd do them tomorrow when I felt more prepared, but tomorrow I'd feel just as scared. It wasn't laziness, it was fear of confirming that I didn't understand the material well enough. The gamified tasks were easier to approach because they felt lower stakes.
Q16: Deadlines were both motivating and terrifying. They'd force me to finally engage with material I'd been avoiding, but the panic wasn't productive. I'd cram right before due dates, which meant my LMS activity probably spiked erratically. The gamified deadline reminders helped a bit because they framed things as challenges rather than threats.
Q17: When I got feedback, it affected me a lot. Positive feedback made me want to engage more and try harder. But when feedback pointed out mistakes, sometimes it made me want to retreat and avoid that type of task. I'm not great at bouncing back from criticism, so feedback was a double-edged sword for my engagement.
Q18: The course structure with gamified elements helped me stay engaged more than a traditional setup would have. Having clear milestones and rewards gave me something to aim for. But the sheer volume of content across seven courses meant I couldn't give each one the attention it deserved. The structure helped, but it couldn't solve the overload problem.
Q19: The gamification made engagement easier, genuinely. The badges and progress tracking appealed to my need for tangible accomplishments. But the heavy reliance on text-based materials made things harder for me as a kinesthetic learner. More interactive, hands-on activities would have helped me engage more deeply with the actual content.
Q20: I can relate to that pattern actually. Sometimes you're trying really hard, logging in constantly, watching videos, participating in discussions, but you're so overwhelmed or confused that you can't translate that activity into completed assignments. High activity can mean someone is struggling and searching for understanding, not that they're succeeding.
Q21: That's not really my experience since I had high activity but still failed. But I imagine some students just study more efficiently offline or already have strong backgrounds in the material. They might not need to engage much online because they're learning through other means. The LMS doesn't capture everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns, like when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend on videos. But they miss the quality of engagement, whether someone is actually understanding or just going through motions. They definitely miss offline studying, emotional states, and whether someone is learning or just anxious and clicking around.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It means being mentally present, asking questions, trying to connect concepts to real applications. For me, it also means doing things with my hands, practicing physically. True engagement is active participation in your own learning, not passive consumption.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how hard I was trying despite failing. It wouldn't show the hours I spent practicing offline with notebooks and diagrams. It wouldn't capture that I was genuinely motivated but struggling with resilience and resources. My engagement was real, it just didn't translate into the results the system measures.
